title: Deity

infobox.value: or **god** is a [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural") being considered to have authority over some aspect of the universe and/or life, with many also being considered as [sacred](/wiki/Sacred "Sacred") and worthy of [worship](/wiki/Worship "Worship").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The *Oxford Dictionary of English* defines *deity* as a god or [goddess](/wiki/Goddess "Goddess"), or anything revered as [divine](/wiki/Divine "Divine").[[3]](#cite_note-Stevenson-3) [C. Scott Littleton](/wiki/C._Scott_Littleton "C. Scott Littleton") defines a deity as "a being with powers greater than those of ordinary humans, but who interacts with humans, positively or negatively, in ways that carry humans to new [levels of consciousness](/wiki/Higher_consciousness "Higher consciousness"), beyond the grounded preoccupations of ordinary life".[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Examples of representations of deities in different cultures; clockwise from upper left: [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Yoruba](/wiki/Yoruba_religion "Yoruba religion"), [Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_religion "Ancient Egyptian religion"), [Maya](/wiki/Maya_religion "Maya religion"), [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto"), [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), [Graeco](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_religion "Ancient Greek religion")-[Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Roman_religion "Ancient Roman religion"), and [Inca](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Inca_Empire "Religion in the Inca Empire")

A **deity** or **god** is a [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural") being considered to have authority over some aspect of the universe and/or life, with many also being considered as [sacred](/wiki/Sacred "Sacred") and worthy of [worship](/wiki/Worship "Worship").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The *Oxford Dictionary of English* defines *deity* as a god or [goddess](/wiki/Goddess "Goddess"), or anything revered as [divine](/wiki/Divine "Divine").[[3]](#cite_note-Stevenson-3) [C. Scott Littleton](/wiki/C._Scott_Littleton "C. Scott Littleton") defines a deity as "a being with powers greater than those of ordinary humans, but who interacts with humans, positively or negatively, in ways that carry humans to new [levels of consciousness](/wiki/Higher_consciousness "Higher consciousness"), beyond the grounded preoccupations of ordinary life".[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Religions can be categorized by how many deities they worship. [Monotheistic](/wiki/Monotheism "Monotheism") religions accept only one deity (predominantly referred to as "[God](/wiki/God "God")"),[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) whereas [polytheistic](/wiki/Polytheism "Polytheism") religions accept multiple deities.[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Henotheistic](/wiki/Henotheism "Henotheism") religions accept one [supreme deity](/wiki/God "God") without denying other deities, considering them as aspects of the same divine principle.[[8]](#cite_note-routledge-8)[[9]](#cite_note-ross73-9) [Nontheistic religions](/wiki/Nontheistic_religion "Nontheistic religion") deny any supreme eternal [creator deity](/wiki/Creator_deity "Creator deity"), but may accept a [pantheon](/wiki/Pantheon_(religion) "Pantheon (religion)") of deities which live, die and may be reborn like any other being.[[10]](#cite_note-Keown-10): 35–37[[11]](#cite_note-Bullivant-11): 357–358

Although most monotheistic religions traditionally envision their god as [omnipotent](/wiki/Omnipotence "Omnipotence"), [omnipresent](/wiki/Omnipresence "Omnipresence"), [omniscient](/wiki/Omniscience "Omniscience"), [omnibenevolent](/wiki/Omnibenevolence "Omnibenevolence"), and [eternal](/wiki/Immortality "Immortality"),[[12]](#cite_note-Taliaferro-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Trigger2003-13) none of [these qualities](/wiki/God#Specific_characteristics "God") are essential to the definition of a "deity"[[14]](#cite_note-Hood-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Trigger-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Murdoch-16) and various cultures have conceptualized their deities differently.[[14]](#cite_note-Hood-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Trigger-15) Monotheistic religions typically refer to their god in masculine terms,[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-OBrien2-18): 96 while other religions refer to their deities in a variety of ways—male, female, [hermaphroditic](/wiki/Hermaphrodite "Hermaphrodite"), or genderless.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Many cultures—including the ancient [Mesopotamians](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), [Egyptians](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), [Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), and [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples")—have personified [natural phenomena](/wiki/List_of_natural_phenomena "List of natural phenomena"), variously as either deliberate causes or effects.[[22]](#cite_note-Malandra-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Fløistad-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) Some [Avestan](/wiki/Avesta "Avesta") and [Vedic](/wiki/Vedas "Vedas") deities were viewed as ethical concepts.[[22]](#cite_note-Malandra-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Fløistad-23) In [Indian religions](/wiki/Indian_religions "Indian religions"), deities have been envisioned as manifesting within the temple of every living being's body, as sensory organs and mind.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) Deities are envisioned as a form of existence ([Saṃsāra](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%83s%C4%81ra "Saṃsāra")) after [rebirth](/wiki/Reincarnation "Reincarnation"), for human beings who gain merit through an ethical life, where they become [guardian deities](/wiki/Tutelary_deity "Tutelary deity") and live blissfully in [heaven](/wiki/Heaven "Heaven"), but are also subject to death when their merit is lost.[[10]](#cite_note-Keown-10): 35–38[[11]](#cite_note-Bullivant-11): 356–359

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

Main articles: [Dyeus](/wiki/Dyeus "Dyeus"), [Deus](/wiki/Deus "Deus"), [God (word)](/wiki/God_(word) "God (word)"), and [Deva (Hinduism)](/wiki/Deva_(Hinduism) "Deva (Hinduism)")

The English language word *deity* derives from [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *deité*,[[28]](#cite_note-28)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *deitatem* (nominative *deitas*) or "divine nature", coined by [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") from *[deus](/wiki/Deus "Deus")* ("god"). Deus is related through a common [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language "Proto-Indo-European language") (PIE) origin to *[\*deiwos](/wiki/Dyeus "Dyeus")*.[[29]](#cite_note-29) This root yields the ancient Indian word *[Deva](/wiki/Deva_(Hinduism) "Deva (Hinduism)")* meaning "to gleam, a shining one", from \*div- "to shine", as well as [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") *dios* "[divine](/wiki/Divinity "Divinity")" and [Zeus](/wiki/Zeus "Zeus"); and Latin *deus* "god" ([Old Latin](/wiki/Old_Latin "Old Latin") *deivos*).[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-etymonline1-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Mallory-32): 230–31 Deva is masculine, and the related feminine equivalent is [devi](/wiki/Devi "Devi").[[33]](#cite_note-Monier-Williams-33): 496 Etymologically, the cognates of *Devi* are Latin *dea* and Greek *thea*.[[34]](#cite_note-HawleyWulff1998-34) In [Old Persian](/wiki/Old_Persian "Old Persian"), *[daiva-](/wiki/Daeva "Daeva")* means "[demon](/wiki/Demon "Demon"), evil god",[[31]](#cite_note-etymonline1-31) while in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") it means the opposite, referring to the "heavenly, divine, terrestrial things of high excellence, exalted, shining ones".[[33]](#cite_note-Monier-Williams-33): 496[[35]](#cite_note-Klostermaier-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Mallory2-36)

The closely linked term "[god](/wiki/God_(word) "God (word)")" refers to "supreme being, deity", according to Douglas Harper,[[37]](#cite_note-Etymonline-37) and is derived from [Proto-Germanic](/wiki/Proto-Germanic_language "Proto-Germanic language") \**guthan*, from PIE \**ghut-*, which means "that which is invoked".[[32]](#cite_note-Mallory-32): 230–231 *Guth* in the [Irish language](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language") means "voice". The term \**ghut-* is also the source of [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic") *zovo* ("to call"), Sanskrit *huta-* ("invoked", an epithet of [Indra](/wiki/Indra "Indra")), from the root \**gheu(e)-* ("to call, invoke."),[[37]](#cite_note-Etymonline-37)

An alternate etymology for the term "god" comes from the Proto-Germanic [Gaut](/wiki/Gaut "Gaut"), which traces it to the PIE root \**ghu-to-* ("poured"), derived from the root \**gheu-* ("to pour, pour a [libation](/wiki/Libation "Libation")"). The term \**gheu-* is also the source of the Greek *khein* "to pour".[[37]](#cite_note-Etymonline-37) Originally the word "god" and its other Germanic cognates were [neuter](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") nouns but shifted to being generally masculine under the influence of Christianity in which the [god is typically seen as male](/wiki/Gender_of_God "Gender of God").[[32]](#cite_note-Mallory-32): 230–231[[37]](#cite_note-Etymonline-37) In contrast, all ancient [Indo-European cultures](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_society "Proto-Indo-European society") and [mythologies](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_religion "Proto-Indo-European religion") recognized both masculine and feminine deities.[[36]](#cite_note-Mallory2-36)

## Definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Definitions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/NASA-HS201427a-HubbleUltraDeepField2014-20140603.jpg/250px-NASA-HS201427a-HubbleUltraDeepField2014-20140603.jpg)](/wiki/File:NASA-HS201427a-HubbleUltraDeepField2014-20140603.jpg)

[Pantheists](/wiki/Pantheism "Pantheism") believe that the universe itself and everything in it forms a single, all-encompassing deity.[[38]](#cite_note-Pearsall-38)[[39]](#cite_note-Edwards-39)

There is no universally accepted consensus on what a deity is, and concepts of deities vary considerably across cultures.[[18]](#cite_note-OBrien2-18): 69–74[[40]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Linguistics-40) [Huw Owen](/wiki/Huw_Owen "Huw Owen") states that the term "deity or god or its equivalent in other languages" has a bewildering range of meanings and significance.[[41]](#cite_note-Owen-41): vii–ix It has ranged from "infinite transcendent being who created and lords over the universe" (God), to a "finite entity or experience, with special significance or which evokes a special feeling" (god), to "a concept in religious or philosophical context that relates to nature or magnified beings or a supra-mundane realm", to "numerous other usages".[[41]](#cite_note-Owen-41): vii–ix

A deity is typically conceptualized as a supernatural or divine concept, manifesting in ideas and knowledge, in a form that combines excellence in some or all aspects, wrestling with weakness and questions in other aspects, [heroic](/wiki/Hero "Hero") in outlook and actions, yet tied up with emotions and desires.[[42]](#cite_note-Gupta-42)[[43]](#cite_note-Gupta2012-43) In other cases, the deity is a principle or reality such as the idea of "soul". The [Upanishads](/wiki/Upanishad "Upanishad") of Hinduism, for example, characterize [Atman](/wiki/Atman_(Hinduism) "Atman (Hinduism)") (soul, self) as *deva* (deity), thereby asserting that the *deva* and eternal supreme principle ([Brahman](/wiki/Brahman "Brahman")) is part of every living creature, that this soul is spiritual and divine, and that to realize self-knowledge is to know the supreme.[[44]](#cite_note-Cohen2008-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

[Theism](/wiki/Theism "Theism") is the belief in the existence of one or more deities.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) [Polytheism](/wiki/Polytheism "Polytheism") is the belief in and worship of multiple deities,[[49]](#cite_note-Libbrecht-49) which are usually assembled into a [pantheon](/wiki/Pantheon_(religion) "Pantheon (religion)") of gods and [goddesses](/wiki/Goddess "Goddess"), with accompanying [rituals](/wiki/Ritual "Ritual").[[49]](#cite_note-Libbrecht-49) In most polytheistic religions, the different gods and goddesses are representations of forces of nature or [ancestral principles](/wiki/Ancestral_worship "Ancestral worship"), and can be viewed either as autonomous or as aspects or [emanations](/wiki/Emanationism "Emanationism") of a creator God or [transcendental](/wiki/Transcendence_(religion) "Transcendence (religion)") [absolute principle](/wiki/Absolute_(philosophy) "Absolute (philosophy)") ([monistic](/wiki/Monism "Monism") theologies), which manifests [immanently](/wiki/Immanence "Immanence") in nature.[[49]](#cite_note-Libbrecht-49) [Henotheism](/wiki/Henotheism "Henotheism") accepts the existence of more than one deity, but considers all deities as equivalent representations or aspects of the same divine principle, the highest.[[9]](#cite_note-ross73-9)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[8]](#cite_note-routledge-8)[[51]](#cite_note-51) [Monolatry](/wiki/Monolatry "Monolatry") is the belief that many deities exist, but that only one of these deities may be validly worshipped.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

[Monotheism](/wiki/Monotheism "Monotheism") is the belief that only one deity exists.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-odccmono-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] A monotheistic deity, known as "God", is usually described as [omnipotent](/wiki/Omnipotence "Omnipotence"), [omnipresent](/wiki/Omnipresence "Omnipresence"), [omniscient](/wiki/Omniscience "Omniscience"), [omnibenevolent](/wiki/Omnibenevolence "Omnibenevolence") and [eternal](/wiki/Immortality "Immortality").[[61]](#cite_note-Swinburne-61) However, not all deities have been regarded this way[[14]](#cite_note-Hood-14)[[16]](#cite_note-Murdoch-16)[[62]](#cite_note-Beck2005-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) and an entity does not need to be almighty, omnipresent, omniscient, omnibenevolent or eternal to qualify as a deity.[[14]](#cite_note-Hood-14)[[16]](#cite_note-Murdoch-16)[[62]](#cite_note-Beck2005-62)

[Deism](/wiki/Deism "Deism") is the belief that only one deity exists, who created the universe, but does not usually intervene in the resulting world.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Deism was particularly popular among western intellectuals during the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) [Pantheism](/wiki/Pantheism "Pantheism") is the belief that the universe itself is God[[38]](#cite_note-Pearsall-38) or that everything composes an all-encompassing, [immanent](/wiki/Immanence "Immanence") deity.[[39]](#cite_note-Edwards-39) [Pandeism](/wiki/Pandeism "Pandeism") is an intermediate position between these, proposing that the creator became a pantheistic universe.[[69]](#cite_note-69) [Panentheism](/wiki/Panentheism "Panentheism") is the belief that [divinity](/wiki/Divinity "Divinity") pervades the universe, but that it also [transcends](/wiki/Transcendence_(religion) "Transcendence (religion)") the universe.[[70]](#cite_note-70) [Agnosticism](/wiki/Agnosticism "Agnosticism") is the position that it is impossible to know for certain whether a deity of any kind exists.[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) [Atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") is the non-belief in the existence of any deity.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

## Prehistoric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prehistoric")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Seated_Woman_of_%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk_on_black_background.jpg/250px-Seated_Woman_of_%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk_on_black_background.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seated_Woman_of_%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk_on_black_background.jpg)

Statuette of a [nude, corpulent, seated woman](/wiki/Seated_Woman_of_%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Seated Woman of Çatalhöyük") flanked by two [felines](/wiki/Felidae "Felidae") from [Çatalhöyük](/wiki/%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Çatalhöyük"), dating to c. 6000 BCE, thought by most archaeologists to represent a goddess of some kind[[75]](#cite_note-mellart181-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Further information: [Prehistoric religion](/wiki/Prehistoric_religion "Prehistoric religion")

Scholars infer the probable existence of deities in the prehistoric period from inscriptions and prehistoric arts such as [cave drawings](/wiki/Cave_painting "Cave painting"), but it is unclear what these sketches and paintings are and why they were made.[[77]](#cite_note-77) Some engravings or sketches show animals, hunters or rituals.[[78]](#cite_note-Ruether-78) It was once common for archaeologists to interpret virtually every prehistoric female figurine as a representation of a single, primordial goddess, the ancestor of historically attested goddesses such as [Inanna](/wiki/Inanna "Inanna"), [Ishtar](/wiki/Ishtar "Ishtar"), [Astarte](/wiki/Astarte "Astarte"), [Cybele](/wiki/Cybele "Cybele"), and [Aphrodite](/wiki/Aphrodite "Aphrodite");[[79]](#cite_note-Lesure-79) this approach has now generally been discredited.[[79]](#cite_note-Lesure-79) Modern archaeologists now generally recognize that it is impossible to conclusively identify any prehistoric figurines as representations of any kind of deities, let alone goddesses.[[79]](#cite_note-Lesure-79) Nonetheless, it is possible to evaluate ancient representations on a case-by-case basis and rate them on how likely they are to represent deities.[[79]](#cite_note-Lesure-79) The [Venus of Willendorf](/wiki/Venus_of_Willendorf "Venus of Willendorf"), a female figurine found in Europe and dated to about 25,000 BCE has been interpreted by some as an exemplar of a prehistoric female deity.[[78]](#cite_note-Ruether-78) A number of probable representations of deities have been discovered at ['Ain Ghazal](/wiki/Ayn_Ghazal_(archaeological_site) "Ayn Ghazal (archaeological site)")[[79]](#cite_note-Lesure-79) and the works of art uncovered at [Çatalhöyük](/wiki/%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Çatalhöyük") reveal references to what is probably a complex mythology.[[79]](#cite_note-Lesure-79)

## Religions and cultures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Religions and cultures")]

### Sub-Saharan African

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Sub-Saharan African")]

Main articles: [List of African mythological figures](/wiki/List_of_African_mythological_figures "List of African mythological figures"), [Traditional African religion](/wiki/Traditional_African_religion "Traditional African religion"), [Afro-American religion](/wiki/Afro-American_religion "Afro-American religion"), and [Orisha](/wiki/Orisha "Orisha")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Mus%C3%A9e_africain_Lyon_130909_02.jpg/250px-Mus%C3%A9e_africain_Lyon_130909_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mus%C3%A9e_africain_Lyon_130909_02.jpg)

Yoruba deity from Nigeria

Diverse African cultures developed theology and concepts of deities over their history. In [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") and neighboring [West African countries](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa"), for example, two prominent deities (locally called *[Òrìṣà](/wiki/Orisha "Orisha")*)[[80]](#cite_note-Osun-80) are found in the [Yoruba religion](/wiki/Yoruba_religion "Yoruba religion"), namely the god [Ogun](/wiki/Ogun "Ogun") and the goddess [Osun](/wiki/Oshun "Oshun").[[80]](#cite_note-Osun-80) Ogun is the primordial masculine deity as well as the archdivinity and guardian of occupations such as tools making and use, metal working, hunting, war, protection and ascertaining equity and justice.[[81]](#cite_note-Barnes-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) Osun is an equally powerful primordial feminine deity and a multidimensional guardian of fertility, water, maternal, health, social relations, love and peace.[[80]](#cite_note-Osun-80) Ogun and Osun traditions were brought into the Americas on [slave ships](/wiki/Slave_ship "Slave ship"). They were preserved by the Africans in their plantation communities, and their festivals continue to be observed.[[80]](#cite_note-Osun-80)[[81]](#cite_note-Barnes-81)

In [Southern African cultures](/wiki/Southern_Africa "Southern Africa"), a similar masculine-feminine deity combination has appeared in other forms, particularly as the Moon and Sun deities.[[83]](#cite_note-83) One Southern African cosmology consists of *Hieseba* or *Xuba* (deity, god), *Gaune* (evil spirits) and *Khuene* (people). The *Hieseba* includes *Nladiba* (male, creator sky god) and *Nladisara* (females, Nladiba's two wives). The Sun (female) and the Moon (male) deities are viewed as offspring of *Nladiba* and two *Nladisara*. The Sun and Moon are viewed as manifestations of the supreme deity, and worship is timed and directed to them.[[84]](#cite_note-84) In other African cultures the Sun is seen as male, while the Moon is female, both symbols of the godhead.[[85]](#cite_note-Lynch-85): 199–120 In [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe"), the supreme deity is [androgynous](/wiki/Androgyny "Androgyny") with male-female aspects, envisioned as the giver of rain, treated simultaneously as the god of darkness and light and is called *Mwari Shona*.[[85]](#cite_note-Lynch-85): 89 In the [Lake Victoria](/wiki/Lake_Victoria "Lake Victoria") region, the term for a deity is *Lubaale*, or alternatively *Jok*.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### Ancient Near Eastern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ancient Near Eastern")]

Main article: [Religions of the ancient Near East](/wiki/Religions_of_the_ancient_Near_East "Religions of the ancient Near East")

#### Egyptian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Egyptian")]

Main articles: [Ancient Egyptian deities](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_deities "Ancient Egyptian deities"), [Egyptian mythology](/wiki/Egyptian_mythology "Egyptian mythology"), and [Ancient Egyptian religion](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_religion "Ancient Egyptian religion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/La_Tombe_de_Horemheb_cropped.jpg/250px-La_Tombe_de_Horemheb_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_Tombe_de_Horemheb_cropped.jpg)

Egyptian tomb painting showing the gods [Osiris](/wiki/Osiris "Osiris"), [Anubis](/wiki/Anubis "Anubis"), and [Horus](/wiki/Horus "Horus"), who are among the major deities in ancient Egyptian religion[[87]](#cite_note-Pinch-87)

[Ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") culture revered numerous deities. Egyptian records and inscriptions list the names of many whose nature is unknown and make vague references to other unnamed deities.[[88]](#cite_note-Wilkinson1-88): 73 [Egyptologist](/wiki/Egyptology "Egyptology") [James P. Allen](/wiki/James_Peter_Allen "James Peter Allen") estimates that more than 1,400 deities are named in Egyptian texts,[[89]](#cite_note-Allen-89) whereas Christian Leitz offers an estimate of "thousands upon thousands" of Egyptian deities.[[90]](#cite_note-Johnston-90): 393–394 Their terms for deities were *nṯr* (god), and feminine *nṯrt* (goddess);[[91]](#cite_note-Baines-91): 42 however, these terms may also have applied to any being – spirits and deceased human beings, but not demons – who in some way were outside the sphere of everyday life.[[92]](#cite_note-Assmann-92): 216[[91]](#cite_note-Baines-91): 62 Egyptian deities typically had an associated cult, role and mythologies.[[92]](#cite_note-Assmann-92): 7–8, 83

Around 200 deities are prominent in the [Pyramid texts](/wiki/Pyramid_Texts "Pyramid Texts") and ancient temples of Egypt, many [zoomorphic](/wiki/Zoomorphism "Zoomorphism"). Among these, were *Min* (fertility god), *Neith* (creator goddess), *Anubis*, *Atum*, *Bes*, *Horus*, *Isis*, *Ra*, *Meretseger*, *Nut*, *Osiris*, *Shu*, *Sia* and *Thoth*.[[87]](#cite_note-Pinch-87): 11–12 Most Egyptian deities represented natural phenomenon, physical objects or social aspects of life, as hidden immanent forces within these phenomena.[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) The deity *Shu*, for example represented air; the goddess *Meretseger* represented parts of the earth, and the god *Sia* represented the abstract powers of perception.[[95]](#cite_note-Hart-95): 91, 147 Deities such as *Ra* and *Osiris* were associated with the judgement of the dead and their care during the afterlife.[[87]](#cite_note-Pinch-87): 26–28 Major gods often had multiple roles and were involved in multiple phenomena.[[95]](#cite_note-Hart-95): 85–86

The first written evidence of deities are from early 3rd millennium BCE, likely emerging from prehistoric beliefs.[[96]](#cite_note-96) However, deities became systematized and sophisticated after the formation of an Egyptian state under the [Pharaohs](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh") and their treatment as [sacred kings](/wiki/Sacred_king "Sacred king") who had exclusive rights to interact with the gods, in the later part of the 3rd millennium BCE.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[88]](#cite_note-Wilkinson1-88): 12–15 Through the early centuries of the common era, as Egyptians interacted and traded with neighboring cultures, foreign deities were adopted and venerated.[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[90]](#cite_note-Johnston-90): 160

#### Levantine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Levantine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Zeus_Yahweh.jpg/250px-Zeus_Yahweh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zeus_Yahweh.jpg)

The [God on the Winged Wheel coin](/wiki/God_on_the_Winged_Wheel_coin "God on the Winged Wheel coin"), a 4th-century BCE [drachm](/wiki/Yehud_coinage "Yehud coinage") (quarter [shekel](/wiki/Shekel "Shekel")) coin from the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"), possibly representing [Yahweh](/wiki/Yahweh "Yahweh") seated on a winged and wheeled sun-throne

Main articles: [Ancient Canaanite religion](/wiki/Ancient_Canaanite_religion "Ancient Canaanite religion"), [Origins of Judaism](/wiki/Origins_of_Judaism "Origins of Judaism"), [Ancient Semitic religion](/wiki/Ancient_Semitic_religion "Ancient Semitic religion"), [Yahweh](/wiki/Yahweh "Yahweh"), [Second Temple Judaism](/wiki/Second_Temple_Judaism "Second Temple Judaism"), and [History of ancient Israel and Judah](/wiki/History_of_ancient_Israel_and_Judah "History of ancient Israel and Judah")

The ancient [Canaanites](/wiki/Canaanites "Canaanites") were polytheists who believed in a pantheon of deities,[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99)[[100]](#cite_note-CooganSmith-100)[[101]](#cite_note-MarkSSmith2002-101) the chief of whom was the god [El](/wiki/El_(deity) "El (deity)"), who ruled alongside his consort [Asherah](/wiki/Asherah "Asherah") and their [seventy sons](/wiki/Sons_of_God "Sons of God").[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99): 22–24[[100]](#cite_note-CooganSmith-100)[[101]](#cite_note-MarkSSmith2002-101) [Baal](/wiki/Baal "Baal") was the god of storm, rain, vegetation and fertility,[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99): 68–127 while his consort [Anat](/wiki/Anat "Anat") was the goddess of war[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99): 131, 137–139 and [Astarte](/wiki/Astarte "Astarte"), the [West Semitic](/wiki/West_Semitic_languages "West Semitic languages") equivalent to [Ishtar](/wiki/Ishtar "Ishtar"), was the goddess of love.[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99): 146–149 The people of the [Kingdoms of Israel](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Israel_(Samaria) "Kingdom of Israel (Samaria)") and [Judah](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Judah "Kingdom of Judah") originally believed in these deities,[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99)[[101]](#cite_note-MarkSSmith2002-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102) alongside their own [national god](/wiki/National_god "National god") Yahweh.[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) El later became [syncretized](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") with [Yahweh](/wiki/Yahweh "Yahweh"), who took over El's role as the head of the pantheon,[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99): 13–17 with Asherah as his divine consort[[105]](#cite_note-Niehr-105): 45[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99): 146 and the "sons of El" as his offspring.[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99): 22–24 During the later years of the [Kingdom of Judah](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Judah "Kingdom of Judah"), a [monolatristic](/wiki/Monolatry "Monolatry") faction rose to power insisting that only Yahweh was fit to be worshipped by the people of Judah.[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99): 229–233 Monolatry became enforced during the reforms of [King Josiah](/wiki/Josiah "Josiah") in 621 BCE.[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99): 229 Finally, during the national crisis of the [Babylonian captivity](/wiki/Babylonian_captivity "Babylonian captivity"), some [Judahites](/wiki/Ioudaios "Ioudaios") began to teach that deities aside from Yahweh were not just unfit to be worshipped, but did not exist.[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[41]](#cite_note-Owen-41): 4 The "sons of El" were demoted from deities to [angels](/wiki/Angels_in_Judaism "Angels in Judaism").[[99]](#cite_note-Day-99): 22

Main articles: [List of Mesopotamian deities](/wiki/List_of_Mesopotamian_deities "List of Mesopotamian deities"), [Ancient Mesopotamian religion](/wiki/Ancient_Mesopotamian_religion "Ancient Mesopotamian religion"), and [Sumerian religion](/wiki/Sumerian_religion "Sumerian religion")

Ancient [Mesopotamian culture](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") in southern [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") had numerous *[dingir](/wiki/Dingir "Dingir")* (deities, gods and goddesses).[[18]](#cite_note-OBrien2-18): 69–74[[40]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Linguistics-40) Mesopotamian deities were almost exclusively anthropomorphic.[[107]](#cite_note-Black-107): 93[[18]](#cite_note-OBrien2-18): 69–74[[108]](#cite_note-108) They were thought to possess extraordinary powers[[107]](#cite_note-Black-107): 93 and were often envisioned as being of tremendous physical size.[[107]](#cite_note-Black-107): 93 They were generally immortal,[[107]](#cite_note-Black-107): 93 but a few of them, particularly [Dumuzid](/wiki/Dumuzid_the_Shepherd "Dumuzid the Shepherd"), [Geshtinanna](/wiki/Geshtinanna "Geshtinanna"), and [Gugalanna](/wiki/Gugalanna "Gugalanna") were said to have either died or visited the underworld.[[107]](#cite_note-Black-107): 93 Both male and female deities were widely venerated.[[107]](#cite_note-Black-107): 93

In the Sumerian pantheon, deities had multiple functions, which included presiding over procreation, rains, irrigation, agriculture, destiny, and justice.[[18]](#cite_note-OBrien2-18): 69–74 The gods were fed, clothed, entertained, and worshipped to prevent natural catastrophes as well as to prevent social chaos such as pillaging, rape, or atrocities.[[18]](#cite_note-OBrien2-18): 69–74[[109]](#cite_note-Nemet-109): 186[[107]](#cite_note-Black-107): 93 Many of the Sumerian deities were [patron guardians](/wiki/Tutelary_deity "Tutelary deity") of [city-states](/wiki/City-state "City-state").[[109]](#cite_note-Nemet-109)

The most important deities in the Sumerian pantheon were known as the [Anunnaki](/wiki/Anunnaki "Anunnaki"),[[110]](#cite_note-Kramer1963-110) and included deities known as the "seven gods who decree": [An](/wiki/Anu "Anu"), [Enlil](/wiki/Enlil "Enlil"), [Enki](/wiki/Enki "Enki"), [Ninhursag](/wiki/Ninhursag "Ninhursag"), [Nanna](/wiki/Sin_(mythology) "Sin (mythology)"), [Utu](/wiki/Utu "Utu") and [Inanna](/wiki/Inanna "Inanna").[[110]](#cite_note-Kramer1963-110) After the conquest of Sumer by [Sargon of Akkad](/wiki/Sargon_of_Akkad "Sargon of Akkad"), many Sumerian deities were [syncretized](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") with [East Semitic](/wiki/East_Semitic "East Semitic") ones.[[109]](#cite_note-Nemet-109) The goddess Inanna, syncretized with the East Semitic Ishtar, became popular,[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-Wolkstein-112): xviii, xv[[109]](#cite_note-Nemet-109): 182[[107]](#cite_note-Black-107): 106–09 with temples across Mesopotamia.[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[107]](#cite_note-Black-107): 106–09

The Mesopotamian mythology of the first millennium BCE treated [Anšar](/wiki/Anshar "Anshar") (later [Aššur](/wiki/Ashur_(god) "Ashur (god)")) and [Kišar](/wiki/Kishar "Kishar") as primordial deities.[[114]](#cite_note-114) [Marduk](/wiki/Marduk "Marduk") was a significant god among the Babylonians. He rose from an obscure deity of the third millennium BCE to become one of the most important deities in the Mesopotamian pantheon of the first millennium BCE. The Babylonians worshipped Marduk as creator of heaven, earth and humankind, and as their [national god](/wiki/National_god "National god").[[18]](#cite_note-OBrien2-18): 62, 73[[115]](#cite_note-115) Marduk's iconography is zoomorphic and is most often found in Middle Eastern archaeological remains depicted as a "snake-dragon" or a "human-animal hybrid".[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-VDT-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118)

#### Germanic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Germanic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Kirkby_Stephen_Stone_by_Petersen.jpg/250px-Kirkby_Stephen_Stone_by_Petersen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kirkby_Stephen_Stone_by_Petersen.jpg)

The Kirkby Stephen Stone, discovered in [Kirkby Stephen](/wiki/Kirkby_Stephen "Kirkby Stephen"), England, depicts a bound figure, who some have theorized may be the Germanic god [Loki](/wiki/Loki "Loki").

Main articles: [List of Germanic deities](/wiki/List_of_Germanic_deities "List of Germanic deities"), [Germanic paganism](/wiki/Germanic_paganism "Germanic paganism"), [Germanic mythology](/wiki/Germanic_mythology "Germanic mythology"), [Common Germanic deities](/wiki/Common_Germanic_deities "Common Germanic deities"), [Æsir](/wiki/%C3%86sir "Æsir"), and [Vanir](/wiki/Vanir "Vanir")

In [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages"), the terms cognate with '[god](/wiki/God_(word) "God (word)")' such as [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language"): *god* and [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse_language "Old Norse language"): *guð* were originally neuter but became masculine, as in modern Germanic languages, after [Christianisation](/wiki/Christianisation_of_the_Germanic_peoples "Christianisation of the Germanic peoples") due their use in referring to the [Christian god](/wiki/Christian_god "Christian god").[[119]](#cite_note-gudą-119)

In [Norse mythology](/wiki/Norse_mythology "Norse mythology"), *[Æsir](/wiki/%C3%86sir "Æsir")* (singular *áss* or *ǫ́ss*) are the principal group of gods,[[120]](#cite_note-áss-120) while the term *ásynjur* (singular *ásynja*) refers specifically to the female *Æsir*.[[121]](#cite_note-ásynja-121) These terms, states John Lindow, may be ultimately rooted in the Indo-European root for "breath" (as in "life giving force"), and are cognate with [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language"): *os* (a [heathen](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_paganism "Anglo-Saxon paganism") god) and [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_language "Gothic language"): *anses*.[[122]](#cite_note-Lindow-122): 49–50

Another group of deities found in Norse mythology are termed as *[Vanir](/wiki/Vanir "Vanir")*, and are associated with fertility. The *Æsir* and the *Vanir* [went to war](/wiki/%C3%86sir%E2%80%93Vanir_War "Æsir–Vanir War"), according to the Nordic sources. The account in *[Ynglinga saga](/wiki/Ynglinga_saga "Ynglinga saga")* describes the Æsir–Vanir War ending in truce and ultimate reconciliation of the two into a single group of gods, after both sides chose peace, exchanged ambassadors (hostages),[[123]](#cite_note-Warner-123): 181 and intermarried.[[122]](#cite_note-Lindow-122): 52–53[[124]](#cite_note-124)

The Norse mythology describes the cooperation after the war, as well as differences between the *Æsir* and the *Vanir* which were considered scandalous by the other side.[[123]](#cite_note-Warner-123): 181 The goddess [Freyja](/wiki/Freyja "Freyja") of the *Vanir* taught magic to the *Æsir*, while the two sides discover that while *Æsir* forbid [mating between siblings](/wiki/Incest_between_siblings "Incest between siblings"), *Vanir* accepted such mating.[[123]](#cite_note-Warner-123): 181[[125]](#cite_note-125)[[126]](#cite_note-126)

[Temples](/wiki/Heathen_hof "Heathen hof") hosting [images](/wiki/Anthropomorphic_wooden_cult_figurines_of_Central_and_Northern_Europe "Anthropomorphic wooden cult figurines of Central and Northern Europe") of Germanic gods (such as [Thor](/wiki/Thor "Thor"), [Odin](/wiki/Odin "Odin") and [Freyr](/wiki/Freyr "Freyr")), as well as pagan worship rituals, continued in [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") into the 12th century, according to historical records. It has been proposed that over time, Christian equivalents were substituted for the Germanic deities to help suppress [paganism](/wiki/Old_Nordic_religion "Old Nordic religion") as part of the [Christianisation of the Germanic peoples](/wiki/Christianisation_of_the_Germanic_peoples "Christianisation of the Germanic peoples").[[123]](#cite_note-Warner-123): 187–188 Worship of the Germanic gods has been revived in the modern period as part of the [new religious movement](/wiki/New_religious_movement "New religious movement") of [Heathenry](/wiki/Heathenry_(new_religious_movement) "Heathenry (new religious movement)").[[127]](#cite_note-127)

Main articles: [List of Greek deities](/wiki/List_of_Greek_deities "List of Greek deities"), [Greek mythology](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology"), [Ancient Greek religion](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_religion "Ancient Greek religion"), and [Twelve Olympians](/wiki/Twelve_Olympians "Twelve Olympians")

The [ancient Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") revered both gods and goddesses.[[128]](#cite_note-Martin-128) These continued to be revered through the early centuries of the common era, and many of the Greek deities inspired and were adopted as part of much larger pantheon of Roman deities.[[129]](#cite_note-Gagarin-129): 91–97 The Greek religion was polytheistic, but had no centralized church, nor any sacred texts.[[129]](#cite_note-Gagarin-129): 91–97 The deities were largely associated with myths and they represented natural phenomena or aspects of human behavior.[[128]](#cite_note-Martin-128)[[129]](#cite_note-Gagarin-129): 91–97

Several Greek deities probably trace back to more ancient Indo-European traditions, since the gods and goddesses found in distant cultures are mythologically comparable and are [cognates](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate").[[32]](#cite_note-Mallory-32): 230–231[[130]](#cite_note-Burkert-130): 15–19 [Eos](/wiki/Eos "Eos"), the Greek goddess of the dawn, for instance, is cognate to Indic *[Ushas](/wiki/Ushas "Ushas")*, Roman *[Aurora](/wiki/Aurora_(mythology) "Aurora (mythology)")* and Latvian *[Auseklis](/wiki/Auseklis "Auseklis")*.[[32]](#cite_note-Mallory-32): 230–232 [Zeus](/wiki/Zeus "Zeus"), the Greek king of gods, is cognate to Latin *[Iūpiter](/wiki/Jupiter_(mythology) "Jupiter (mythology)")*, Old German *[Ziu](/wiki/T%C3%BDr "Týr")*, and Indic *[Dyaus](/wiki/Dyaus_Pita "Dyaus Pita")*, with whom he shares similar mythologies.[[32]](#cite_note-Mallory-32): 230–232[[131]](#cite_note-131) Other deities, such as [Aphrodite](/wiki/Aphrodite "Aphrodite"), originated from the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East").[[132]](#cite_note-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133)[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135)

Greek deities varied locally, but many shared panhellenic themes, celebrated similar festivals, rites, and ritual grammar.[[136]](#cite_note-136) The most important deities in the Greek pantheon were the [Twelve Olympians](/wiki/Twelve_Olympians "Twelve Olympians"): Zeus, [Hera](/wiki/Hera "Hera"), [Poseidon](/wiki/Poseidon "Poseidon"), [Athena](/wiki/Athena "Athena"), [Apollo](/wiki/Apollo "Apollo"), [Artemis](/wiki/Artemis "Artemis"), Aphrodite, [Hermes](/wiki/Hermes "Hermes"), [Demeter](/wiki/Demeter "Demeter"), [Dionysus](/wiki/Dionysus "Dionysus"), [Hephaestus](/wiki/Hephaestus "Hephaestus"), and [Ares](/wiki/Ares "Ares").[[130]](#cite_note-Burkert-130): 125–170 Other important Greek deities included [Hestia](/wiki/Hestia "Hestia"), [Hades](/wiki/Hades "Hades") and [Heracles](/wiki/Heracles "Heracles").[[129]](#cite_note-Gagarin-129): 96–97 These deities later inspired the *[Dii Consentes](/wiki/Dii_Consentes "Dii Consentes")* galaxy of Roman deities.[[129]](#cite_note-Gagarin-129): 96–97

Besides the Olympians, the Greeks also worshipped various local deities.[[130]](#cite_note-Burkert-130): 170–181[[137]](#cite_note-Pollard-137) Among these were the goat-legged god [Pan](/wiki/Pan_(mythology) "Pan (mythology)") (the guardian of shepherds and their flocks), [Nymphs](/wiki/Nymph "Nymph") ([nature spirits](/wiki/List_of_nature_deities "List of nature deities") associated with particular landforms), [Naiads](/wiki/Naiad "Naiad") (who dwelled in springs), [Dryads](/wiki/Dryad "Dryad") (who were spirits of the trees), [Nereids](/wiki/Nereid "Nereid") (who inhabited the sea), river gods, [satyrs](/wiki/Satyr "Satyr") (a class of lustful male nature spirits), and others. The dark powers of the underworld were represented by the [Erinyes](/wiki/Erinyes "Erinyes") (or Furies), said to pursue those guilty of crimes against blood-relatives.[[137]](#cite_note-Pollard-137)

The Greek deities, like those in many other Indo-European traditions, were anthropomorphic. [Walter Burkert](/wiki/Walter_Burkert "Walter Burkert") describes them as "persons, not abstractions, ideas or concepts".[[130]](#cite_note-Burkert-130): 182 They had fantastic abilities and powers; each had some unique expertise and, in some aspects, a specific and flawed personality.[[138]](#cite_note-Campbell-138): 52 They were not omnipotent and could be injured in some circumstances.[[139]](#cite_note-139) Greek deities led to cults, were used politically and inspired [votive offerings](/wiki/Votive_offerings "Votive offerings") for favors such as bountiful crops, healthy family, victory in war, or peace for a loved one recently deceased.[[129]](#cite_note-Gagarin-129): 94–95[[140]](#cite_note-140)

#### Roman

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Roman")]

Main articles: [List of Roman deities](/wiki/List_of_Roman_deities "List of Roman deities"), [Roman mythology](/wiki/Roman_mythology "Roman mythology"), [Religion in ancient Rome](/wiki/Religion_in_ancient_Rome "Religion in ancient Rome"), and [Capitoline Triad](/wiki/Capitoline_Triad "Capitoline Triad")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/MANNapoli_6705_creation_of_the_man_sarcophagus.jpg/500px-MANNapoli_6705_creation_of_the_man_sarcophagus.jpg)](/wiki/File:MANNapoli_6705_creation_of_the_man_sarcophagus.jpg)

4th-century [Roman sarcophagus](/wiki/Ancient_Roman_sarcophagi "Ancient Roman sarcophagi") depicting the creation of man by [Prometheus](/wiki/Prometheus "Prometheus"), with major Roman deities Jupiter, Neptune, Mercury, Juno, Apollo, Vulcan watching

The Roman pantheon had numerous deities, both Greek and non-Greek.[[129]](#cite_note-Gagarin-129): 96–97 The more famed deities, found in the mythologies and the 2nd millennium CE European arts, have been the anthropomorphic deities syncretized with the Greek deities. These include the six gods and six goddesses: Venus, Apollo, Mars, Diana, Minerva, Ceres, Vulcan, Juno, Mercury, Vesta, Neptune, Jupiter (Jove, Zeus); as well Bacchus, Pluto and Hercules.[[129]](#cite_note-Gagarin-129): 96–97[[141]](#cite_note-141) The non-Greek major deities include Janus, Fortuna, Vesta, Quirinus and Tellus (mother goddess, probably most ancient).[[129]](#cite_note-Gagarin-129): 96–97[[142]](#cite_note-142) Some of the non-Greek deities had likely origins in more ancient European culture such as the ancient Germanic religion, while others may have been borrowed, for political reasons, from neighboring trade centers such as those in the [Minoan](/wiki/Minoan_civilization "Minoan civilization") or [ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") civilization.[[143]](#cite_note-143)[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145)

The Roman deities, in a manner similar to the ancient Greeks, inspired community festivals, rituals and sacrifices led by *flamines* (priests, pontifs), but priestesses (Vestal Virgins) were also held in high esteem for maintaining sacred fire used in the votive rituals for deities.[[129]](#cite_note-Gagarin-129): 100–101 Deities were also maintained in home shrines (*lararium*), such as Hestia honored in homes as the goddess of fire hearth.[[129]](#cite_note-Gagarin-129): 100–101[[146]](#cite_note-Kristensen-146) This Roman religion held reverence for sacred fire, and this is also found in Hebrew culture (Leviticus 6), Vedic culture's Homa, ancient Greeks and other cultures.[[146]](#cite_note-Kristensen-146)

Ancient Roman scholars such as Varro and Cicero wrote treatises on the nature of gods of their times.[[147]](#cite_note-Cicero-147) Varro stated, in his *Antiquitates Rerum Divinarum*, that it is the superstitious man who fears the gods, while the truly religious person venerates them as parents.[[147]](#cite_note-Cicero-147) Cicero, in his *[Academica](/wiki/Academica_(Cicero) "Academica (Cicero)")*, praised Varro for this and other insights.[[147]](#cite_note-Cicero-147) According to Varro, there have been three accounts of deities in the Roman society: the mythical account created by poets for theatre and entertainment, the civil account used by people for veneration as well as by the city, and the natural account created by the philosophers.[[148]](#cite_note-Barfield-148) The best state is, adds Varro, where the civil theology combines the poetic mythical account with the philosopher's.[[148]](#cite_note-Barfield-148) The Roman deities continued to be revered in Europe through the era of Constantine, and past 313 CE when he issued the Edict of Toleration.[[138]](#cite_note-Campbell-138): 118–120

### Native American

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Native American")]

#### Inca

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Inca")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Inti_Raymi2.jpg/250px-Inti_Raymi2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inti_Raymi2.jpg)

Inti Raymi, a winter solstice festival of the Inca people, reveres *Inti*, the sun deity—offerings include round bread and maize beer.

Main articles: [Inca mythology](/wiki/Inca_mythology "Inca mythology"), [Religion in the Inca Empire](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Inca_Empire "Religion in the Inca Empire"), and [Inca religion in Cusco](/wiki/Inca_religion_in_Cusco "Inca religion in Cusco")

The [Inca culture](/wiki/Inca_Empire "Inca Empire") has believed in *[Viracocha](/wiki/Viracocha "Viracocha")* (also called *Pachacutec*) as the [creator deity](/wiki/Creator_deity "Creator deity").[[149]](#cite_note-Roza-149): 27–30[[150]](#cite_note-Littleton-150): 726–729 *Viracocha* has been an abstract deity to Inca culture, one who existed before he created space and time.[[151]](#cite_note-151) All other deities of the Inca people have corresponded to elements of nature.[[149]](#cite_note-Roza-149)[[150]](#cite_note-Littleton-150): 726–729 Of these, the most important ones have been *[Inti](/wiki/Inti "Inti")* ([sun deity](/wiki/Solar_deity "Solar deity")) responsible for agricultural prosperity and as the father of the first Inca king, and *Mama Qucha* the goddess of the sea, lakes, rivers and waters.[[149]](#cite_note-Roza-149) *Inti* in some mythologies is the son of *Viracocha* and *Mama Qucha*.[[149]](#cite_note-Roza-149)[[152]](#cite_note-Sherman-152)

> **Inca Sun deity festival**
>
> Oh creator and Sun and Thunder,  
> be forever copious,  
> do not make us old,  
> let all things be at peace,  
> multiply the people,  
> and let there be food,  
> and let all things be fruitful.

—Inti Raymi prayers[[153]](#cite_note-Parker-153)

Inca people have revered many male and female deities. Among the feminine deities have been *Mama Kuka* (goddess of joy), *Mama Ch'aska* (goddess of dawn), *Mama Allpa* (goddess of harvest and earth, sometimes called *Mama Pacha* or *[Pachamama](/wiki/Pachamama "Pachamama")*), [Mama Killa](/wiki/Mama_Killa "Mama Killa") ([moon goddess](/wiki/List_of_lunar_deities "List of lunar deities")) and *Mama Sara* (goddess of grain).[[152]](#cite_note-Sherman-152)[[149]](#cite_note-Roza-149): 31–32 During and after the imposition of Christianity during [Spanish colonialism](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), the Inca people retained their original beliefs in deities through [syncretism](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism"), where they overlay the Christian God and teachings over their original beliefs and practices.[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[156]](#cite_note-Kuznar-156) The male deity *Inti* became accepted as the Christian God, but the Andean rituals centered around Inca deities have been retained and continued thereafter into the modern era by the Inca people.[[156]](#cite_note-Kuznar-156)[[157]](#cite_note-Fagan-157)

#### Maya and Aztec

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Maya and Aztec")]

Main articles: [List of Maya gods and supernatural beings](/wiki/List_of_Maya_gods_and_supernatural_beings "List of Maya gods and supernatural beings"), [Maya religion](/wiki/Maya_religion "Maya religion"), [List of Aztec gods and supernatural beings](/wiki/List_of_Aztec_gods_and_supernatural_beings "List of Aztec gods and supernatural beings"), and [Aztec mythology](/wiki/Aztec_mythology "Aztec mythology")

In [Maya culture](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization"), *[Kukulkan](/wiki/Kukulkan "Kukulkan")* has been the supreme [creator deity](/wiki/Creator_deity "Creator deity"), also revered as the god of [reincarnation](/wiki/Reincarnation "Reincarnation"), water, fertility and wind.[[150]](#cite_note-Littleton-150): 797–798 The Maya people built [step pyramid temples](/wiki/Mesoamerican_pyramids "Mesoamerican pyramids") to honor *Kukulkan*, aligning them to the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun")'s position on the spring [equinox](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox").[[150]](#cite_note-Littleton-150): 843–844 Other deities found at Maya archaeological sites include *[Xib Chac](/wiki/Chac-Xib-Chac "Chac-Xib-Chac")*—the benevolent male rain deity, and *[Ixchel](/wiki/Ixchel "Ixchel")*—the benevolent female earth, weaving and pregnancy goddess.[[150]](#cite_note-Littleton-150): 843–844 The [Maya calendar](/wiki/Maya_calendar "Maya calendar") had 18 months, each with 20 days (and five unlucky days of *Uayeb*); each month had a presiding deity, who inspired social rituals, special trading markets and community festivals.[[157]](#cite_note-Fagan-157)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Quetzalc%C3%B3atl_5.jpg/250px-Quetzalc%C3%B3atl_5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quetzalc%C3%B3atl_5.jpg)

Quetzalcoatl in the [Codex Borgia](/wiki/Codex_Borgia "Codex Borgia")

A deity with aspects similar to *Kulkulkan* in the Aztec culture has been called *[Quetzalcoatl](/wiki/Quetzalcoatl "Quetzalcoatl")*.[[150]](#cite_note-Littleton-150): 797–798 However, states Timothy Insoll, the Aztec ideas of deity remain poorly understood. What has been assumed is based on what was constructed by [Christian missionaries](/wiki/Christian_mission "Christian mission"). The deity concept was likely more complex than these historical records.[[158]](#cite_note-Insoll-158) In [Aztec culture](/wiki/Aztec_culture "Aztec culture"), there were hundred of deities, but many were henotheistic [incarnations](/wiki/Incarnation "Incarnation") of one another (similar to the [avatar](/wiki/Avatar "Avatar") concept of Hinduism). Unlike Hinduism and other cultures, Aztec deities were usually not anthropomorphic, and were instead zoomorphic or hybrid icons associated with spirits, natural phenomena or forces.[[158]](#cite_note-Insoll-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159) The Aztec deities were often represented through ceramic figurines, revered in home shrines.[[158]](#cite_note-Insoll-158)[[160]](#cite_note-160)

### Polynesian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Polynesian")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Wooden_idols_of_Polynesia_%281830%29.jpg/250px-Wooden_idols_of_Polynesia_%281830%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wooden_idols_of_Polynesia_(1830).jpg)

Deities of Polynesia carved from wood (bottom two are demons)

Main article: [Polynesian narrative](/wiki/Polynesian_narrative "Polynesian narrative")

The [Polynesian people](/wiki/Polynesians "Polynesians") developed a theology centered on numerous deities, with clusters of islands having different names for the same idea. There are great deities found across the Pacific Ocean. Some deities are found widely, and there are many local deities whose worship is limited to one or a few islands or sometimes to isolated villages on the same island.[[161]](#cite_note-Williamson-161): 5–6

The [Māori people](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people"), of what is now New Zealand, called the supreme being as *[Io](/wiki/Io_Matua_Kore "Io Matua Kore")*, who is also referred elsewhere as *Iho-Iho*, *Io-Mataaho*, *Io Nui*, *Te Io Ora*, *Io Matua Te Kora* among other names.[[162]](#cite_note-Coulter-162): 239 The *Io* deity has been revered as the original uncreated creator, with power of life, with nothing outside or beyond him.[[162]](#cite_note-Coulter-162): 239Other deities in the Polynesian pantheon include *[Tangaloa](/wiki/Tangaroa "Tangaroa")* (god who created men),[[161]](#cite_note-Williamson-161): 37–38 *[La'a Maomao](/wiki/La%27a_Maomao "La'a Maomao")* (god of winds), *[Tu-Matauenga](/wiki/T%C5%ABmatauenga "Tūmatauenga")* or *[Ku](/wiki/K%C5%AB "Kū")* (god of war), *[Tu-Metua](/wiki/Tu-metua "Tu-metua")* (mother goddess), *[Kane](/wiki/K%C4%81ne "Kāne")* (god of procreation) and *[Rangi](/wiki/Rangi_and_Papa "Rangi and Papa")* (sky god father).[[162]](#cite_note-Coulter-162): 261, 284, 399, 476

The Polynesian deities have been part of a sophisticated theology, addressing questions of creation, the nature of existence, guardians in daily lives as well as during wars, natural phenomena, good and evil spirits, priestly rituals, as well as linked to the journey of the souls of the dead.[[161]](#cite_note-Williamson-161): 6–14, 37–38, 113, 323

### Abrahamic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Abrahamic")]

#### Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Christianity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/%C5%A0ven%C4%8Diausioji_Trejyb%C4%97.jpg/250px-%C5%A0ven%C4%8Diausioji_Trejyb%C4%97.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C5%A0ven%C4%8Diausioji_Trejyb%C4%97.jpg)

*Holy Trinity* (1756–1758) by [Szymon Czechowicz](/wiki/Szymon_Czechowicz "Szymon Czechowicz"), showing [God the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"), [God the Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son"), and the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity"), all of whom are revered in Christianity as a single deity

Main articles: [God in Christianity](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity"), [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity"), [God the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"), [God the Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son"), [Jesus in Christianity](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity"), [Holy Spirit in Christianity](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity"), [Names of God in Christianity](/wiki/Names_of_God_in_Christianity "Names of God in Christianity"), and [Christian theology](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology")

Christianity is a monotheistic religion in which most mainstream congregations and denominations accept the concept of the Holy [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity").[[163]](#cite_note-Emery-163): 233–234 Modern orthodox Christians believe that the Trinity is composed of three equal, [cosubstantial](/wiki/Consubstantiality "Consubstantiality") persons: [God the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"), [God the Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son"), and the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit "Holy Spirit").[[163]](#cite_note-Emery-163): 233–234 The first person to describe the persons of the Trinity as *homooúsios* (ὁμοούσιος; "of the same substance") was the [Church Father](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers") Origen.[[164]](#cite_note-La_Due-164) Although most early Christian theologians (including [Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen")) were [Subordinationists](/wiki/Subordinationism "Subordinationism"),[[165]](#cite_note-Badcock-165) who believed that the Father was superior to the Son and the Son superior to the Holy Spirit,[[164]](#cite_note-La_Due-164)[[166]](#cite_note-166)[[167]](#cite_note-167) this belief was condemned as heretical by the [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") in the fourth century, which declared that all three persons of the Trinity are equal.[[165]](#cite_note-Badcock-165) Christians regard the universe as an element in God's actualization[[163]](#cite_note-Emery-163): 273 and the Holy Spirit is seen as the divine essence that is "the unity and relation of the [Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father") and the Son".[[163]](#cite_note-Emery-163): 273 According to George Hunsinger, the doctrine of the Trinity justifies worship in a Church, wherein Jesus Christ is deemed to be a full deity with the [Christian cross](/wiki/Christian_cross "Christian cross") as his icon.[[163]](#cite_note-Emery-163): 296

The theological examination of Jesus Christ, of divine grace in incarnation, his non-transferability and completeness has been a historic topic. For example, the [Council of Chalcedon](/wiki/Council_of_Chalcedon "Council of Chalcedon") in 451 CE declared that in "one person Jesus Christ, fullness of deity and fullness of humanity are united, the union of the natures being such that they can neither be divided nor confused".[[168]](#cite_note-168) Jesus Christ, according to the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), is the self-disclosure of the one, true God, both in his teaching and in his person; Christ, in Christian faith, is considered the incarnation of God.[[41]](#cite_note-Owen-41): 4, 29[[169]](#cite_note-169)[[170]](#cite_note-170)

#### Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Islam")]

Main articles: [Allah](/wiki/Allah "Allah"), [Ilah](/wiki/Ilah "Ilah"), [God in Islam](/wiki/God_in_Islam "God in Islam"), and [Names of God in Islam](/wiki/Names_of_God_in_Islam "Names of God in Islam")

[Ilah](/wiki/Ilah "Ilah"), **ʾIlāh** ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): إله; plural: آلهة **ʾālihah**), is an [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") word meaning "god".[[171]](#cite_note-Saritoprak-171)[[172]](#cite_note-Cornell-172) It appears in the name of the monotheistic god of Islam as [Allah](/wiki/Allah "Allah") (**[al-Lāh](/wiki/Allah "Allah")**).[[173]](#cite_note-173)[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-gardet-allah-175) which literally means "the god" in Arabic.[[171]](#cite_note-Saritoprak-171)[[172]](#cite_note-Cornell-172) Islam is strictly monotheistic[[176]](#cite_note-Hammer-176) and the first statement of the *[shahada](/wiki/Shahada "Shahada")*, or Muslim confession of faith, is that "there is no **ʾilāh** (deity) but Allah (God)",[[177]](#cite_note-Yust-177) who is perfectly unified and utterly indivisible.[[176]](#cite_note-Hammer-176)[[177]](#cite_note-Yust-177)[[178]](#cite_note-178)

The term [Allah](/wiki/Allah "Allah") is used by Muslims for God. The Persian word *[Khuda](/wiki/Khuda "Khuda")* ([Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"): خدا) can be translated as god, lord or king, and is also used today to refer to [God in Islam](/wiki/God_in_Islam "God in Islam") by Persian, [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"), [Tat](/wiki/Tat_language_(Caucasus) "Tat language (Caucasus)") and [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurdish_languages "Kurdish languages") speakers. The [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages") word for god is [Tengri](/wiki/Tengri "Tengri"); it exists as *Tanrı* in [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language").

#### Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Judaism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Tetragrammaton_scripts.svg/250px-Tetragrammaton_scripts.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tetragrammaton_scripts.svg)

The tetragrammaton in [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenician_alphabet "Phoenician alphabet") (12th century BCE to 150 BCE), [Paleo-Hebrew](/wiki/Paleo-Hebrew_alphabet "Paleo-Hebrew alphabet") (10th century BCE to 135 CE), and square [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_alphabet "Hebrew alphabet") (3rd century BCE to present) scripts

Main articles: [God in Judaism](/wiki/God_in_Judaism "God in Judaism"), [Yahweh](/wiki/Yahweh "Yahweh"), [Tetragrammaton](/wiki/Tetragrammaton "Tetragrammaton"), [Elohim](/wiki/Elohim "Elohim"), and [Names of God in Judaism](/wiki/Names_of_God_in_Judaism "Names of God in Judaism")

Judaism affirms the existence of one God (Yahweh, or YHWH), who is not abstract, but He who revealed himself throughout Jewish history particularly during the Exodus and the Exile.[[41]](#cite_note-Owen-41): 4 Judaism reflects a monotheism that gradually arose, was affirmed with certainty in the sixth century "Second Isaiah", and has ever since been the axiomatic basis of its theology.[[41]](#cite_note-Owen-41): 4

The classical presentation of Judaism has been as a monotheistic faith that rejected deities and related idolatry.[[179]](#cite_note-Terry-179) However, states Breslauer, modern scholarship suggests that idolatry was not absent in biblical faith, and it resurfaced multiple times in Jewish religious life.[[179]](#cite_note-Terry-179) The rabbinic texts and other secondary Jewish literature suggest worship of material objects and natural phenomena through the medieval era, while the core teachings of Judaism maintained monotheism.[[179]](#cite_note-Terry-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

According to Aryeh Kaplan, God is always referred to as "He" in Judaism, "not to imply that the concept of sex or gender applies to God", but because "there is no neuter in the Hebrew language, and the Hebrew word for God is a masculine noun" as he "is an active rather than a passive creative force".[[181]](#cite_note-181)

#### Mandaeism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Mandaeism")]

Main article: [Hayyi Rabbi](/wiki/Hayyi_Rabbi "Hayyi Rabbi")

Further information: [Mandaeism](/wiki/Mandaeism "Mandaeism")

In [Mandaeism](/wiki/Mandaeism "Mandaeism"), *[Hayyi Rabbi](/wiki/Hayyi_Rabbi "Hayyi Rabbi")* (lit=The Great Life), or 'The Great Living God',[[182]](#cite_note-Nashmi-182) is the supreme God from which all things [emanate](/wiki/Emanationism "Emanationism"). He is also known as 'The First Life', since during the creation of the material world, [Yushamin](/wiki/Yushamin "Yushamin") emanated from Hayyi Rabbi as the "Second Life."[[183]](#cite_note-Buckley_2002-183) "The principles of the Mandaean doctrine: the belief of the only one great God, Hayyi Rabbi, to whom all absolute properties belong; He created all the worlds, formed the soul through his power, and placed it by means of angels into the human body. So He created [Adam and Eve](/wiki/Adam_and_Eve "Adam and Eve"), the first man and woman."[[184]](#cite_note-184) Mandaeans recognize God to be the eternal, creator of all, the one and only in domination who has no partner.[[185]](#cite_note-Routledge-185)

#### Anitism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Anitism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Visayan_tenegre_horn_hilt_closeup.JPG/120px-Visayan_tenegre_horn_hilt_closeup.JPG)](/wiki/File:Visayan_tenegre_horn_hilt_closeup.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Anitos_of_Northern_tribes_%28c._1900%2C_Philippines%29.jpg/120px-Anitos_of_Northern_tribes_%28c._1900%2C_Philippines%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anitos_of_Northern_tribes_(c._1900,_Philippines).jpg)
