Further information: [Indigenous Philippine folk religions](/wiki/Indigenous_Philippine_folk_religions "Indigenous Philippine folk religions"), [Philippine mythology](/wiki/Philippine_mythology "Philippine mythology"), and [List of Philippine mythological figures](/wiki/List_of_Philippine_mythological_figures "List of Philippine mythological figures")

[Anitism](/wiki/Indigenous_Philippine_folk_religions "Indigenous Philippine folk religions"), composed of an array of indigenous religions from the Philippines, has multiple pantheons of deities. There are more than a hundred different [ethnic groups in the Philippines](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_the_Philippines "Ethnic groups in the Philippines"), each having their own supreme deity or deities. Each supreme deity or deities normally rules over a pantheon of deities, contributing to the sheer diversity of deities in Anitism.[[186]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-186)The supreme deity or deities of ethnic groups are almost always the most notable.[[186]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-186)

For example, [Bathala](/wiki/Bathala "Bathala") is the Tagalog supreme deity,[[187]](#cite_note-187) Mangechay is the Kapampangan supreme deity,[[188]](#cite_note-188) [Malayari](/wiki/Mayari "Mayari") is the Sambal supreme deity,[[189]](#cite_note-189) Melu is the Blaan supreme deity,[[190]](#cite_note-190) Kaptan is the Bisaya supreme deity,[[191]](#cite_note-191) and so on.

#### Buddhism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Buddhism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Ssangbongsa_11-05266.JPG/120px-Ssangbongsa_11-05266.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ssangbongsa_11-05266.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/A_Chinese_deity_with_sword_accompanied_by_a_tiger._Gouache_Wellcome_V0047141.jpg/120px-A_Chinese_deity_with_sword_accompanied_by_a_tiger._Gouache_Wellcome_V0047141.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Chinese_deity_with_sword_accompanied_by_a_tiger._Gouache_Wellcome_V0047141.jpg)

Left: Buddhist deity in [Ssangbongsa](/wiki/Ssangbongsa "Ssangbongsa") in South Korea; Right: Chinese deity adopted into Buddhism

Further information: [Creator in Buddhism](/wiki/Creator_in_Buddhism "Creator in Buddhism") and [Buddhist deities](/wiki/Buddhist_deities "Buddhist deities")

Although Buddhists do not believe in a [creator deity](/wiki/Creator_deity "Creator deity"),[[192]](#cite_note-McClelland-192) deities are an essential part of Buddhist teachings about cosmology, [rebirth](/wiki/Rebirth_(Buddhism) "Rebirth (Buddhism)"), and [saṃsāra](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%83s%C4%81ra_(Buddhism) "Saṃsāra (Buddhism)").[[192]](#cite_note-McClelland-192) Buddhist deities (such as *[devas](/wiki/Deva_(Buddhism) "Deva (Buddhism)")* and *[bodhisattvas](/wiki/Bodhisattva "Bodhisattva")*) are believed to reside in a pleasant, heavenly realm within [Buddhist cosmology](/wiki/Buddhist_cosmology "Buddhist cosmology"), which is typically subdivided into twenty six sub-realms.[[193]](#cite_note-Trainor-193)[[192]](#cite_note-McClelland-192)[[10]](#cite_note-Keown-10): 35

Devas are numerous, but they are still mortal;[[193]](#cite_note-Trainor-193) they live in the heavenly realm, then die and are reborn like all other beings.[[193]](#cite_note-Trainor-193) A rebirth in the heavenly realm is believed to be the result of leading an ethical life and accumulating very good [karma](/wiki/Karma "Karma").[[193]](#cite_note-Trainor-193) A *deva* does not need to work, and is able to enjoy in the heavenly realm all pleasures found on Earth. However, the pleasures of this realm lead to attachment (*[upādāna](/wiki/Up%C4%81d%C4%81na "Upādāna")*), lack of spiritual pursuits, and therefore no [nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana "Nirvana").[[10]](#cite_note-Keown-10): 37 Nonetheless, according to Kevin Trainor, the vast majority of Buddhist [lay people](/wiki/Laity "Laity") in countries practicing [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") have historically pursued Buddhist rituals and practices because they are motivated by their potential rebirth into the *deva* realm.[[193]](#cite_note-Trainor-193)[[194]](#cite_note-194)[[195]](#cite_note-195) The *deva* realm in Buddhist practice in Southeast Asia and East Asia, states Keown, include gods found in Hindu traditions such as [Indra](/wiki/Indra "Indra") and [Brahma](/wiki/Brahma "Brahma"), and concepts in [Hindu cosmology](/wiki/Hindu_cosmology "Hindu cosmology") such as [Mount Meru](/wiki/Mount_Meru "Mount Meru").[[10]](#cite_note-Keown-10): 37–38

[Mahayana](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") Buddhism also includes different kinds of deities, such as numerous [Buddhas](/wiki/Buddhahood "Buddhahood"), [bodhisattvas](/wiki/Bodhisattva "Bodhisattva") and [fierce deities](/wiki/Fierce_deities "Fierce deities").

#### Hinduism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Hinduism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Ganesha_Basohli_miniature_circa_1730_Dubost_p73.jpg/250px-Ganesha_Basohli_miniature_circa_1730_Dubost_p73.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ganesha_Basohli_miniature_circa_1730_Dubost_p73.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Saraswati.jpg/250px-Saraswati.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saraswati.jpg)

Left: [Ganesha](/wiki/Ganesha "Ganesha") god of new beginnings, remover of obstacle; Right: [Saraswati](/wiki/Saraswati "Saraswati"), goddess of knowledge and music

Main articles: [Hindu deities](/wiki/Hindu_deities "Hindu deities"), [Deva (Hinduism)](/wiki/Deva_(Hinduism) "Deva (Hinduism)"), [Devi](/wiki/Devi "Devi"), [God in Hinduism](/wiki/God_in_Hinduism "God in Hinduism"), [Ishvara](/wiki/Ishvara "Ishvara"), and [Bhagavan](/wiki/Bhagavan "Bhagavan")

The concept of God varies in [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), it being a diverse system of thought with beliefs spanning [henotheism](/wiki/Henotheism "Henotheism"), [monotheism](/wiki/Monotheism "Monotheism"), [polytheism](/wiki/Polytheism "Polytheism"), [panentheism](/wiki/Panentheism "Panentheism"), [pantheism](/wiki/Pantheism "Pantheism") and [monism](/wiki/Monism "Monism") among others.[[196]](#cite_note-196)[[197]](#cite_note-197)

In the ancient [Vedic](/wiki/Vedas "Vedas") texts of Hinduism, a deity is often referred to as [Deva](/wiki/Deva_(Hinduism) "Deva (Hinduism)") (god) or [Devi](/wiki/Devi "Devi") (goddess).[[33]](#cite_note-Monier-Williams-33): 496[[35]](#cite_note-Klostermaier-35) The root of these terms mean "heavenly, divine, anything of excellence".[[33]](#cite_note-Monier-Williams-33): 492[[35]](#cite_note-Klostermaier-35) Deva is masculine, and the related feminine equivalent is [devi](/wiki/Devi "Devi"). In the earliest Vedic literature, all [supernatural beings](/wiki/Supernatural_being "Supernatural being") are called [Asuras](/wiki/Asura "Asura").[[198]](#cite_note-Hale-198): 5–11, 22, 99–102[[33]](#cite_note-Monier-Williams-33): 121 Over time, those with a benevolent nature become deities and are referred to as *Sura*, Deva or Devi.[[198]](#cite_note-Hale-198): 2–6[[199]](#cite_note-199)

Devas or deities in Hindu texts differ from Greek or Roman [theodicy](/wiki/Theodicy "Theodicy"), states Ray Billington, because many Hindu traditions believe that a human being has the potential to be reborn as a *deva* (or *devi*), by living an ethical life and building up saintly *[karma](/wiki/Karma "Karma")*.[[200]](#cite_note-Billington-200) Such a *deva* enjoys heavenly bliss, till the merit runs out, and then the [soul](/wiki/Soul "Soul") is reborn again into [Saṃsāra](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%83s%C4%81ra "Saṃsāra"). Thus deities are henotheistic manifestations, embodiments and consequence of the virtuous, the noble, the saint-like living in many Hindu traditions.[[200]](#cite_note-Billington-200)

#### Shinto

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Shinto")]

Main article: [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto")

Shinto is [polytheistic](/wiki/Polytheism "Polytheism"), involving the veneration of many deities known as *kami*,[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200223CaliDougill201313-201) or sometimes as *jingi*.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199770Hardacre201731-202) In Japanese, no distinction is made here between singular and plural, and hence the term *kami* refers both to individual *kami* and the collective group of *kami*.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoydWilliams200535CaliDougill201313-203) Although lacking a direct English translation,[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart20048-204) the term *kami* has sometimes been rendered as "god" or "spirit".[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart20042CaliDougill201313-205) The historian of religion [Joseph Kitagawa](/wiki/Joseph_Kitagawa "Joseph Kitagawa") deemed these English translations "quite unsatisfactory and misleading",[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa198736-206) and various scholars urge against translating *kami* into English.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979194Bocking199784-207) In Japanese, it is often said that there are [eight million](/wiki/Indefinite_and_fictitious_numbers#Specific_values_used_as_indefinite "Indefinite and fictitious numbers") *kami*, a term which connotes an infinite number,[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199629Littleton200224-208) and Shinto practitioners believe that they are present everywhere.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre20171-209) They are not regarded as [omnipotent](/wiki/Omnipotence "Omnipotence"), [omniscient](/wiki/Omniscience "Omniscience"), or necessarily [immortal](/wiki/Immortality "Immortality").[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoydWilliams200535Hardacre201752-210)

#### Taoism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Taoism")]

Main articles: [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), [Chinese mythology](/wiki/Chinese_mythology "Chinese mythology"), and [Chinese gods and immortals](/wiki/Chinese_gods_and_immortals "Chinese gods and immortals")

Taoism is a polytheistic religion. The gods and immortals（神仙） believed in by Taoism can be roughly divided into two categories, namely "gods" and "[xian](/wiki/Xian_(Taoism) "Xian (Taoism)")" (immortals). Among them,"Gods" are also called deities and there are many kinds, that is, god of heaven（天神), god of ground（地祇), wuling（物灵: animism, the spirit of all things), god of netherworld（地府神灵), god of human body（人体之神), god of human ghost（人鬼之神）etc. Among these "gods" such as god of heaven（天神), god of ground（地祇), god of netherworld（阴府神灵), god of human body（人体之神） are innate beings.In China, "gods" are often referred to together with "xian". "Xian" (immortals) is acquired the cultivation of the Tao，persons with vast supernatural powers, unpredictable changes and immortality.[[211]](#cite_note-211)

#### Jainism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Jainism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Padmavati.JPG/250px-Padmavati.JPG)](/wiki/File:Padmavati.JPG)

Padmavati, a Jain guardian deity

Main articles: [God in Jainism](/wiki/God_in_Jainism "God in Jainism") and [Deva (Jainism)](/wiki/Deva_(Jainism) "Deva (Jainism)")

[Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism") does not believe in a creator, omnipotent, omniscient, eternal God; however, the cosmology of Jainism incorporates a meaningful [causality](/wiki/Causality "Causality")-driven reality, including four realms of existence (*gati*), one of them being *[deva](/wiki/Deva_(Jainism) "Deva (Jainism)")* (celestial beings, gods).[[11]](#cite_note-Bullivant-11): 351–357 A human being can choose and live an ethical life, such as being non-violent ([ahimsa](/wiki/Ahimsa "Ahimsa")) against all living beings, and thereby gain merit and be reborn as *deva*.[[11]](#cite_note-Bullivant-11): 357–358[[212]](#cite_note-212)

Jain texts reject a trans-cosmic God, one who stands outside of the universe and lords over it, but they state that the world is full of *devas* who are in human-image with sensory organs, with the power of reason, conscious, compassionate and with finite life.[[11]](#cite_note-Bullivant-11): 356–357 Jainism believes in the existence of the [soul](/wiki/Soul "Soul") (Self, [atman](/wiki/J%C4%ABva_(Jainism) "Jīva (Jainism)")) and considers it to have "god-quality", whose knowledge and liberation is the ultimate spiritual goal in both religions. Jains also believe that the spiritual nobleness of perfected souls ([Jina](/wiki/Arihant_(Jainism) "Arihant (Jainism)")) and *devas* make them worship-worthy beings, with powers of guardianship and guidance to better *[karma](/wiki/Karma_in_Jainism "Karma in Jainism")*. In Jain temples or festivals, the Jinas and Devas are revered.[[11]](#cite_note-Bullivant-11): 356–357[[213]](#cite_note-213)

#### Zoroastrianism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Zoroastrianism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Taq-e_Bostan_-_High-relief_of_Ardeshir_II_investiture.jpg/250px-Taq-e_Bostan_-_High-relief_of_Ardeshir_II_investiture.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taq-e_Bostan_-_High-relief_of_Ardeshir_II_investiture.jpg)

Investiture of Sassanid emperor [Shapur II](/wiki/Shapur_II "Shapur II") (center) with [Mithra](/wiki/Mithra "Mithra") (left) and Ahura Mazda (right) at [Taq-e Bostan](/wiki/Taq-e_Bostan "Taq-e Bostan"), [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran")

Main article: [Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda")

[Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda") ([/əˌhʊrəˌmæzdə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"));[[214]](#cite_note-214) is the [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan_language "Avestan language") name for the creator and sole God of [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism").[[215]](#cite_note-Boyce-215) The literal meaning of the word *[Ahura](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Ahura "wikt:Ahura")* is "mighty" or "lord" and  *[Mazda](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Mazda "wikt:Mazda")* is *[wisdom](/wiki/Wisdom "Wisdom")*.[[215]](#cite_note-Boyce-215) [Zoroaster](/wiki/Zoroaster "Zoroaster"), the founder of Zoroastrianism, taught that Ahura Mazda is the most powerful being in all of the existence[[216]](#cite_note-Andrea-216) and the only deity who is worthy of the highest veneration.[[216]](#cite_note-Andrea-216) Nonetheless, Ahura Mazda is not omnipotent because his [evil twin](/wiki/Evil_twin "Evil twin") brother [Angra Mainyu](/wiki/Angra_Mainyu "Angra Mainyu") is nearly as powerful as him.[[216]](#cite_note-Andrea-216) Zoroaster taught that the *[daevas](/wiki/Daeva "Daeva")* were evil spirits created by Angra Mainyu to sow evil in the world[[216]](#cite_note-Andrea-216) and that all people must choose between the goodness of Ahura Mazda and the evil of Angra Mainyu.[[216]](#cite_note-Andrea-216) According to Zoroaster, Ahura Mazda will eventually defeat Angra Mainyu and good will triumph over evil once and for all.[[216]](#cite_note-Andrea-216) Ahura Mazda was the most important deity in the ancient [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire").[[217]](#cite_note-217) He was originally represented anthropomorphically,[[215]](#cite_note-Boyce-215) but, by the end of the [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"), Zoroastrianism had become fully aniconic.[[215]](#cite_note-Boyce-215)

## Local, regional, and universal deities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Local, regional, and universal deities")]

A deity may originate as a local deity, whose realm or influence applies to a specific geographic place.[[a]](#cite_note-218)
Some local deities may become or merge with trans-regional deities[[b]](#cite_note-220)
or even universal ones. Within the Ancient Greek context,  [Pan](/wiki/Pan_(god) "Pan (god)") started as a local Arcadian deity whose influence spread.[[219]](#cite_note-221)
The [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") adopted many regional deities[[220]](#cite_note-222)
from places such as Greece ([Apollo](/wiki/Apollo "Apollo"), for example), Asia ([Cybele](/wiki/Cybele "Cybele"), [Mithras](/wiki/Mithras "Mithras")) and Egypt ([Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis"), [Serapis](/wiki/Serapis "Serapis")) in the process of building a multi-ethnic polity. The  [Romans spread the worship of several non-Roman deities](/wiki/Religion_in_ancient_Rome "Religion in ancient Rome") and  [syncretised](/wiki/Religious_syncretism "Religious syncretism") some of their features. Roman authorities eventually favoured [Yahweh](/wiki/Yahweh "Yahweh"), first associated with a mountain in northwestern Arabia.[[221]](#cite_note-223)
prior to his becoming a tribal deity of the peripatetic Hebrews[[c]](#cite_note-225)
and thus eventually the supreme god of Christianity.

## Skeptical interpretations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deity&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Skeptical interpretations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Lightning_cloud_to_cloud_%28aka%29.jpg/250px-Lightning_cloud_to_cloud_%28aka%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lightning_cloud_to_cloud_(aka).jpg)

The Greek philosopher [Democritus](/wiki/Democritus "Democritus") argued that belief in deities arose when humans observed natural phenomena such as [lightning](/wiki/Lightning "Lightning") and attributed such phenomena to supernatural beings.

See also: [Evolutionary origin of religions](/wiki/Evolutionary_origin_of_religions "Evolutionary origin of religions"), [Evolutionary psychology of religion](/wiki/Evolutionary_psychology_of_religion "Evolutionary psychology of religion"), and [Neurotheology](/wiki/Neurotheology "Neurotheology")

Attempts to rationally explain belief in deities extend at least as far back as [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece").[[130]](#cite_note-Burkert-130): 311–317 The Greek philosopher [Democritus](/wiki/Democritus "Democritus") argued that the concept of deities arose when human beings observed natural phenomena such as lightning, [solar eclipses](/wiki/Solar_eclipse "Solar eclipse"), and the changing of the seasons.[[130]](#cite_note-Burkert-130): 311–317 Later, in the third century BCE, the scholar [Euhemerus](/wiki/Euhemerus "Euhemerus") argued in his book *Sacred History* that the gods were originally flesh-and-blood mortal kings, [posthumously deified](/wiki/Apotheosis "Apotheosis"), and that religion was therefore the continuation of these kings' mortal reigns, a view now known as [Euhemerism](/wiki/Euhemerism "Euhemerism").[[223]](#cite_note-226) [Sigmund Freud](/wiki/Sigmund_Freud "Sigmund Freud") suggested that God-concepts are a projection of one's father.[[224]](#cite_note-Barrett-227)

A tendency to believe in deities and other supernatural beings may be an integral part of human consciousness.[[225]](#cite_note-AtranNorensayan-228)[[226]](#cite_note-Spiegel-229)[[227]](#cite_note-230)[[228]](#cite_note-Barrett2012-231): 2–11 Children are naturally inclined to believe in supernatural entities such as gods, spirits, and demons, even without being introduced into a particular religious tradition.[[228]](#cite_note-Barrett2012-231): 2–11 Humans have an overactive agency-detection system,[[225]](#cite_note-AtranNorensayan-228)[[229]](#cite_note-Guthrie-232)[[228]](#cite_note-Barrett2012-231): 25–27 which has a tendency to conclude that events are caused by intelligent entities, even if they really are not.[[225]](#cite_note-AtranNorensayan-228)[[229]](#cite_note-Guthrie-232) This is a system which may have evolved to cope with threats to the survival of human ancestors:[[225]](#cite_note-AtranNorensayan-228) in the wild, a person who perceived intelligent and potentially dangerous beings everywhere was more likely to survive than a person who failed to perceive actual threats, such as wild animals or human enemies.[[225]](#cite_note-AtranNorensayan-228)[[228]](#cite_note-Barrett2012-231): 2–11 Humans are also inclined to think teleologically and to ascribe meaning and significance to their surroundings, traits which may lead people to believe in a creator-deity.[[230]](#cite_note-Keleman-233) This may have developed as a side-effect of human social intelligence, the ability to discern what other people are thinking.[[230]](#cite_note-Keleman-233)

Stories of encounters with supernatural beings are especially likely to be retold, passed on, and embellished due to their descriptions of standard [ontological](/wiki/Ontological "Ontological") categories (person, artifact, animal, plant, natural object) with counterintuitive properties (humans that are invisible, houses that remember what happened in them, etc.).[[231]](#cite_note-234) As belief in deities spread, humans may have attributed anthropomorphic thought-processes to them,[[232]](#cite_note-boyer-235) leading to the idea of leaving offerings to the gods and praying to them for assistance,[[232]](#cite_note-boyer-235) ideas which are seen in all cultures around the world.[[225]](#cite_note-AtranNorensayan-228)

[Sociologists of religion](/wiki/Sociology_of_religion "Sociology of religion") have proposed that the personality and characteristics of deities may reflect a culture's sense of self-esteem and that a culture projects its revered values into deities and in spiritual terms. The cherished, desired or sought human personality is congruent with the personality it defines to be gods.[[224]](#cite_note-Barrett-227) Lonely and fearful societies tend to invent wrathful, violent, submission-seeking deities, while happier and secure societies tend to invent loving, non-violent, compassionate deities.[[224]](#cite_note-Barrett-227) [Émile Durkheim](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Durkheim "Émile Durkheim") states that gods represent an extension of human social life to include supernatural beings. According to Matt Rossano, God-concepts may be a means of enforcing [morality](/wiki/Morality "Morality") and building more cooperative community-groups.[[233]](#cite_note-supernature-236)
